title_en: The Foundations of Evidence in Islamic Judicial Procedure
content_en:
At-Turuq al-Hukmiyyah's foundational chapter on evidence in Islamic judicial procedure establishes the theoretical basis for Ibn al-Qayyim's argument that Islamic governance has always used a broader range of evidentiary means than the formal rules of testimony alone. His analysis draws on the practice of the Prophet, the four Rightly Guided Caliphs, and the great early judges to demonstrate that substantive justice has always been the goal of Islamic legal procedure.

Ibn al-Qayyim begins by establishing the purposes of Islamic governance and the judiciary. The judge's function is not merely to apply rules mechanically but to discover the truth and render a just judgment. This purpose is established by the Quranic command to judge with justice (al-Nisa 4:58) and the Prophet's instruction 'Judge between people according to what is apparent to you' — indicating that the judge should use all legitimately available means to determine the reality of a dispute.

The formal rules of testimony (shahada) in classical Islamic jurisprudence require: two adult male Muslim witnesses of upright character, whose testimony must agree on the essential facts, who have personally witnessed the event, and who have not been impeached. These requirements ensure that testimony meets a high standard of reliability. But Ibn al-Qayyim notes that these requirements, applied mechanically, can produce injustice: a crime witnessed by one reliable person cannot be prosecuted; a fraudulent contract cannot be overturned if the fraudster has arranged no witnesses; a murderer who killed in private escapes punishment.

Ibn al-Qayyim surveys the prophetic practice and finds that the Prophet regularly used means beyond formal testimony: he considered the oath (yamin) of one party when the evidence was evenly balanced; he used al-qarinah (circumstantial evidence and contextual indicators) extensively; he relied on the practical wisdom of experienced judges to read situations that formal testimony could not capture; and he allowed al-qassamah (oaths sworn by the victim's clan in cases of unresolved homicide) as a means of establishing responsibility in cases where physical evidence was present but witnesses were lacking.

The chapter on the legal status of qarinalah (circumstantial evidence) is one of the most important in At-Turuq al-Hukmiyyah. Ibn al-Qayyim argues that the four schools' formal rules of evidence represent the minimum required for a legally certain judgment, but that strong circumstantial evidence can and should influence judicial decisions in ways that are less final than a formal conviction — affecting, for instance, the allocation of burdens of proof, the permissibility of detention, or the triggering of oaths.

Ibn al-Qayyim draws on historical examples: the Prophet's judgment in favor of the boy who was attacked by an older boy based on the physical evidence; Umar ibn al-Khattab's use of professional knowledge (recognizing symptoms of intoxication without formal testimony to the act of drinking); Ali ibn Abi Talib's legendary reputation for using logical reasoning to reach correct judgments where formal evidence was insufficient. These examples establish that Islamic governance has always valued judicial wisdom and contextual reasoning alongside formal legal procedure.

The chapter concludes by establishing the principle that governs the rest of the work: the Islamic judiciary may use any means of establishing truth that is legitimate (not obtained through violation of rights or dignity) and reliable (having a genuine epistemic connection to the facts in question), subject always to the constraint that rights and punishments prescribed by clear texts cannot be altered by evidentiary innovation.
title_id: Fondasi-Fondasi Pembuktian dalam Prosedur Yudikatif Islam
content_id:
Bab fondasi At-Turuq al-Hukmiyyah tentang pembuktian dalam prosedur yudikatif Islam menetapkan dasar teoritis bagi argumen Ibn al-Qayyim bahwa tata kelola Islam selalu menggunakan rentang cara-cara pembuktian yang lebih luas daripada aturan-aturan formal kesaksian semata. Analisisnya menggali dari praktik Nabi, keempat Khalifah Rasyidin, dan para hakim awal yang agung untuk mendemonstrasikan bahwa keadilan substantif selalu menjadi tujuan prosedur hukum Islam.

Ibn al-Qayyim memulai dengan menetapkan tujuan-tujuan tata kelola Islam dan peradilan. Fungsi hakim bukan sekadar menerapkan aturan-aturan secara mekanis melainkan menemukan kebenaran dan mengeluarkan putusan yang adil. Tujuan ini ditetapkan oleh perintah Qurani untuk berhukum dengan keadilan (an-Nisa 4:58) dan instruksi Nabi 'Putuskan di antara manusia berdasarkan apa yang nampak bagimu' — menunjukkan bahwa hakim seharusnya menggunakan semua cara yang secara sah tersedia untuk menentukan realitas suatu sengketa.

Aturan-aturan formal kesaksian (syahadah) dalam fikih Islam klasik mensyaratkan: dua saksi Muslim laki-laki dewasa yang berkarakter lurus, yang kesaksiannya harus sepakat tentang fakta-fakta esensial, yang telah menyaksikan peristiwa secara pribadi, dan yang tidak dimpeachable. Persyaratan-persyaratan ini memastikan bahwa kesaksian memenuhi standar keandalan yang tinggi. Namun Ibn al-Qayyim mencatat bahwa persyaratan-persyaratan ini, diterapkan secara mekanis, dapat menghasilkan ketidakadilan: kejahatan yang disaksikan oleh satu orang yang dapat dipercaya tidak dapat dituntut; kontrak yang curang tidak dapat dibatalkan jika penipu telah mengatur tidak ada saksi; seorang pembunuh yang membunuh secara pribadi lolos dari hukuman.

Ibn al-Qayyim mensurvei praktik kenabian dan menemukan bahwa Nabi secara rutin menggunakan cara-cara melampaui kesaksian formal: ia mempertimbangkan sumpah (yamin) dari satu pihak ketika bukti seimbang; ia menggunakan al-qarinah (bukti sirkumstansial dan indikator-indikator kontekstual) secara ekstensif; ia mengandalkan kebijaksanaan praktis para hakim berpengalaman untuk membaca situasi-situasi yang tidak dapat ditangkap oleh kesaksian formal; dan ia mengizinkan al-qasamah (sumpah-sumpah yang diucapkan oleh klan korban dalam kasus pembunuhan yang tidak terselesaikan) sebagai cara untuk menetapkan tanggung jawab dalam kasus-kasus di mana bukti fisik ada tetapi saksi tidak ada.

Bab tentang status hukum qarinah (bukti sirkumstansial) adalah salah satu yang paling penting dalam At-Turuq al-Hukmiyyah. Ibn al-Qayyim berpendapat bahwa aturan-aturan pembuktian formal keempat mazhab mewakili minimum yang diperlukan untuk putusan yang pasti secara hukum, tetapi bahwa bukti sirkumstansial yang kuat dapat dan seharusnya mempengaruhi keputusan-keputusan yudikatif dengan cara-cara yang kurang final daripada vonis formal — mempengaruhi, misalnya, alokasi beban-beban pembuktian, kebolehan penahanan, atau pemicu sumpah-sumpah.

Ibn al-Qayyim menggali contoh-contoh historis: putusan Nabi yang mendukung anak laki-laki yang diserang oleh anak yang lebih tua berdasarkan bukti fisik; penggunaan pengetahuan profesional oleh Umar ibn al-Khattab (mengenali gejala-gejala mabuk tanpa kesaksian formal tentang tindakan meminum); reputasi legendaris Ali ibn Abi Thalib dalam menggunakan penalaran logis untuk mencapai putusan-putusan yang benar di mana bukti formal tidak mencukupi. Contoh-contoh ini menetapkan bahwa tata kelola Islam selalu menghargai kebijaksanaan yudikatif dan penalaran kontekstual di samping prosedur hukum formal.

Bab tersebut menyimpulkan dengan menetapkan prinsip yang mengatur sisa karya: peradilan Islam dapat menggunakan cara-cara apa pun dalam menetapkan kebenaran yang sah (tidak diperoleh melalui pelanggaran hak-hak atau martabat) dan dapat diandalkan (memiliki koneksi epistemik yang tulus terhadap fakta-fakta yang dipermasalahkan), selalu tunduk pada kendala bahwa hak-hak dan hukuman-hukuman yang ditetapkan oleh teks-teks jelas tidak dapat diubah oleh inovasi pembuktian.
content_ar: At-Turuq al-Hukmiyyah's foundational الباب on evidence in Islamic judicial procedure establishes the theoretical basis for ابن القيم's argument that Islamic governance has always used a broader range of evidentiary means than the formal rules of testimony alone. His analysis draws on the practice of النبي ﷺ, the four Rightly Guided Caliphs, and the great early judges to demonstrate that substantive العدل has always been the goal of Islamic legal procedure. ابن القيم begins by establishing the purposes of Islamic governance and the judiciary. The judge's function is not merely to apply rules mechanically but to discover the الحق and render a just judgment. This purpose is established by the القرآني command to judge with العدل (al-Nisa 4:58) and النبي ﷺ's instruction 'Judge between people وفقاً لـ what is apparent to you' — indicating that the judge should use all legitimately available means to determine the reality of a dispute. The formal rules of testimony (shahada) in الكلاسيكي Islamic jurisprudence require: two adult male مسلم witnesses of upright الأخلاق, whose testimony must agree on the الجوهري facts, who have personally witnessed the event, and who have not been impeached. These requirements ensure that testimony meets a high standard of reliability. But ابن القيم notes that these requirements, applied mechanically, can produce الظلم: a crime witnessed by one reliable person cannot be prosecuted; a fraudulent contract cannot be overturned إذا the fraudster has arranged no witnesses; a murderer who killed in private escapes العقاب. ابن القيم surveys the prophetic practice and finds that النبي ﷺ regularly used means beyond formal testimony: he considered the oath (yamin) of one party عندما the evidence was evenly balanced; he used al-qarinah (circumstantial evidence and contextual indicators) extensively; he relied on the practical الحكمة of experienced judges to read situations that formal testimony could not capture; and he allowed al-qassamah (oaths sworn by the victim's clan in cases of unresolved homicide) as a means of establishing المسؤولية in cases where physical evidence was present but witnesses were lacking. The الباب on the legal status of qarinalah (circumstantial evidence) is one of the most المهم in At-Turuq al-Hukmiyyah. ابن القيم argues that the four schools' formal rules of evidence represent the minimum required for a legally certain judgment, but that strong circumstantial evidence can and should influence judicial decisions in ways that are less final than a formal conviction — affecting, على سبيل المثال, the allocation of burdens of proof, the permissibility of detention, or the triggering of oaths. ابن القيم draws on historical examples: النبي ﷺ's judgment in النعمة of the boy who was attacked by an older boy based on the physical evidence; عمر ibn al-Khattab's use of professional العلم (recognizing symptom
